metadata.title: Why Cancer Cells Are Harder to Kill With the Drug That Should Kill Them Best

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The idea that each relapse selected a separate sabotage point - BCL-2 overexpression, BAX deletion, IAPs, and more - so the death signal arrives but never executes really stuck with me. I want to think about beating a cell with layered defenses. Can you (1) list all five sabotage points and where each intercepts the mitochondrial death circuit, (2) explain which ones a BH3-mimetic like venetoclax can bypass and which it cannot, and (3) tell me whether combining agents that hit different points restores apoptosis?

beats.narration_text: You're watching an educational A-I template — an explainer on Why Cancer Cells Are Harder to Kill With the Drug That Should Kill Them Best, built so you can remake it for anything in Cancer Biology.

props.command: brutalist explainer-video "Why Cancer Cells Are Harder to Kill With the Drug That Should…"

beats.narration_text: Every beat is tagged in the corner. Those tags let you name a beat to a command-line tool like Claude Code, and it'll swap, add, or delete it — or re-cut the whole thing for a new audience.

lines: # every beat is tagged →  B00  B04  B12 …

beats.narration_text: A patient's leukemia has relapsed three times. Each treatment inflicted DNA damage. Each time, a new population survived.
By the third relapse, these leukemic B cells have one defining feature: they refuse to die.

props.headline: By the third relapse, these leukemic B cells have one defining…

viz.prompt_seed: bone marrow biopsy microscopy slide showing dense clusters of abnormal lymphocytes, clinical pathology, editorial treatment

beats.narration_text: Chemotherapy inflicts enough DNA damage to trigger apoptosis in normal cells. Here the same damage does not kill the leukemic cells. Why does the death signal arrive but not execute?

Apoptosis — programmed cell death — runs through the mitochondria. DNA damage triggers a signal. That signal should open a pore in the mitochondrial membrane. Cytochrome c escapes. Caspases execute death.

viz.note: Intrinsic pathway spine: damage -> signal -> BCL-2 balance -> MOMP -> caspases -> death. All teal, flowing left to right. This is the intact, working circuit.

beats.narration_text: In a healthy cell, that circuit runs cleanly. The leukemic cell has the same circuit. The signal arrives. But something is intercepting it.

viz.note: Same circuit, now with a crimson STOP block appearing at the BCL-2 balance node. Signal arrives left; nothing exits right.

beats.narration_text: Five sabotage points. Each one a separate defense. Each one selected by a chemotherapy exposure.
Sabotage one: BCL-2 overexpression. BCL-2 is a guardian protein. It grabs BAX — the mitochondrial pore-opener — and holds it. No BAX free, no pore, no death.

viz.note: Intrinsic circuit. A crimson BLOCKER labeled 'BCL-2 HIGH' appears at the MOMP node, snapping into place. BAX held, no pore.

beats.narration_text: Sabotage two: BAX is lost. Some cells don't just hold BAX — they delete it. No effector protein at all. Even if BCL-2 is beaten, nothing is there to punch through the membrane.

viz.note: Second crimson BLOCKER labeled 'BAX LOST' appears at effector position. Circuit now has two blocks.

beats.narration_text: Sabotage three: IAPs — inhibitor of apoptosis proteins. Even if MOMP occurs, caspases must activate to execute death. IAPs — specifically XIAP — block them directly at the execution stage.

viz.note: Third crimson BLOCKER labeled 'XIAP' appears at caspase node. Three blocks on circuit now.

beats.narration_text: Sabotage four: PI3K-AKT survival signaling. AKT phosphorylates BAD — a pro-apoptotic protein — inactivating it. Less pressure on BCL-2. The guardian's job gets easier.

viz.note: Fourth crimson BLOCKER labeled 'AKT-BAD' appears at BH3-only sensor node. Four blocks.

beats.narration_text: Sabotage five: p53 mutation. p53 is the hub that links DNA damage to the apoptotic signal. Remove p53, and the damage sensor fires — but the message never reaches the mitochondria.

viz.note: Fifth crimson BLOCKER labeled 'p53 MUT' appears at p53/sensor node. All five blocks now on circuit. The complete sabotage map.

beats.narration_text: Five defenses. Not one, five. A drug that hits one of them leaves four others intact. The death signal still cannot push through.

viz.note: Full five-block circuit held. Pause. GOLD signal arrow attempts to cross from left; stops at first block. Emphasizes redundancy.

beats.narration_text: Here is how selection built it. A leukemic clone. Exposure one: most cells die. BCL-2-high cells survive — selected.

viz.note: Many teal dots (cells); crimson selection pressure; most disappear; one BCL-2-high dot remains enlarged. Label: 'BCL-2 HIGH — SELECTED.' Illustrative.

beats.narration_text: Exposure two: the surviving BCL-2-high clone is hit again. Now cells that have also lost BAX survive. Exposure three: IAP levels rise in the survivors.

viz.note: Staircase continues. Step 2 adds 'BAX LOST'; Step 3 adds 'XIAP UP'. Each step narrows the surviving clone but hardens it. Illustrative.

beats.narration_text: The death signal arrives. It always did. The cancer cell has spent three rounds of chemotherapy building a five-layer wall against it. CANCER BIOLOGY.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Five defenses. Not one, five. A drug that hits one of them leaves four others intact. The death signal still cannot push through. Here is how selection built it. A leukemic clone. Exposure one: most cells die. BCL-2-high cells survive — selected.
